title: Nematic liquid crystal phase in a system of interacting dimers
speaker: Ian Jauslin
abstract: In 1979, O. Heilmann and E.H. Lieb introduced an interacting dimer model with the goal of proving the emergence of a nematic liquid crystal phase in it. In such a phase, dimers spontaneously align, but there is no long range translational order. Heilmann and Lieb proved that dimers do, indeed, align, and conjectured that there is no translational order. I will discuss a recent proof of this conjecture. This is joint work with Elliott H. Lieb.

proof_stages.label: Nematic criterion and interacting dimer model
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates a nematic liquid crystal as orientational long-range order with positional disorder, then introduces the square-lattice monomer-dimer model with an attractive interaction between adjacent aligned dimers as the target system.

proof_stages.label: Known orientational order and remaining conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Heilmann and Lieb's reflection-positivity result gives long-range orientational order for the model, while the lack of positional order remained conjectural, motivating the new theorem and placing it among earlier rod-model results.

proof_stages.label: Gibbs state and vertical phase selection
proof_stages.summary: Finite-volume boxes with boundary strips forbidding horizontal dimers select the vertical extremal phase, and the Gibbs distribution is defined with fugacity z, interaction strength j, infinite-volume limit, and boundary length scale l0 = exp(3j/2) z^{1/2}.

proof_stages.label: High-density strong-coupling theorem
proof_stages.summary: In a regime of large activity and still stronger interaction, the vertical-edge density is asymptotically 1/2, the horizontal-edge density is exponentially small, and truncated two-point functions decay exponentially in an anisotropic Heilmann-Lieb metric, proving orientational order and positional disorder.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a one-directional reference model
proof_stages.summary: The proof compares the full model to the vertical-only monomer-dimer model; the latter factorizes by columns into one-dimensional transfer-matrix problems whose correlation length is l0 and whose densities and vertical correlations supply the benchmark estimates.

proof_stages.label: Loop representation of phase interfaces
proof_stages.summary: Configurations differing from the one-directional model are encoded by loops marking interfaces between vertical and horizontal regions, so the partition-function ratio becomes a loop sum with products of one-directional partition functions on the loop-defined subregions.

proof_stages.label: Peierls bounds and loop interactions
proof_stages.summary: Each interface length loses aligned-dimer interactions and hence carries a Peierls weight roughly exp(-j|loop|/2), while the remaining loop interactions—exponentially decaying one-dimensional correlations and nondecaying many-body orientation effects—are controlled by cluster-expansion and boundary-error estimates.

proof_stages.label: Extraction of observable estimates
proof_stages.summary: The final estimates follow by translating loop costs into observables: a horizontal dimer requires a smallest loop of length six giving exp(-3j), horizontal correlation requires a loop corridor with rate j, and vertical decay retains the one-dimensional rate 1/l0.
